# MongoDB Server — Schema Documentation

> **Read-only metadata export.** No data was modified. The server was only *read*: databases & collections were listed, document counts estimated, indexes read, and a sample of documents per collection was inspected to **infer** the document structure (MongoDB is schemaless, so fields/types come from sampling).

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Host | `10.20.30.1:27017` |
| Server version | MongoDB 7.0.2 |
| Auth | user `mineralView`, authSource `admin` |
| Field inference | Sampled up to 60 docs/collection via `$sample` |
| Counts | `estimatedDocumentCount` (metadata estimate) |

## Databases overview

| # | Database | Collections | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Linkage_data** | 259 | user | Per-county (2026) master **linkage** datasets — wide ~93-field documents that tie together wells, leases, mineral owners, permits and production (the MongoDB counterpart of the Postgres `Linkage_data`). |
| 2 | **MViewNewCommunity** | 28 | user | MineralView community/social platform data (users, posts, groups, interactions and related content). |
| 3 | **ProdMvestPortal** | 20 | user | Production & mineral-value-estimate (MVest) portal — datasets powering per-lease/well production and valuation views. |
| 4 | **EF_SpAnalysis** | 8 | user | Eagle Ford **spatial analysis** — lease production allocation, reserves time-series, nearest-neighbour lease relationships and per-lease well/geometry stats. |
| 5 | **Decline_data_to_web** | 6 | user | **Decline-curve analysis** outputs served to the web — per-well production history, Arps decline fits, EUR/reserves estimates and acreage/spacing data. |
| 6 | **Operator_Directory** | 3 | user | Operator directory datasets (operator profiles and related lookups). |
| 7 | **GeoMapPortal** | 2 | user | Geo/map portal data — well geometry and lease-radius proximity data used for map rendering. |
| 8 | **MineralOwnersInfoDB** | 2 | user | Mineral-owner information datasets. |
| 9 | **Monthly_Reporting_DB** | 2 | user | Monthly reporting datasets/snapshots. |
| 10 | **Data_Allocation** | 1 | user | Production **allocation** analytics — monthly per-well BOE / Arps decline allocation with multi-step (W1–W4) forecast components. |
| 11 | `admin` | 2 | system | MongoDB internal system database (users, roles). |
| 12 | `config` | 1 | system | MongoDB internal configuration database. |
| 13 | `local` | 1 | system | MongoDB internal per-node database (startup log etc.). |

**Totals:** 13 databases, 335 collections.

---

## Database: `Linkage_data`

*Per-county (2026) master **linkage** datasets — wide ~93-field documents that tie together wells, leases, mineral owners, permits and production (the MongoDB counterpart of the Postgres `Linkage_data`).*

### Collections index — 259 collection(s)

## Database: `ProdMvestPortal`

*Production & mineral-value-estimate (MVest) portal — datasets powering per-lease/well production and valuation views.*

### Collections index — 20 collection(s)

| Collection | Est. docs | Fields | Summary |
|---|---|---|---|
| [`Activity`](#prodmvestportal-activity) | 22,995 | 58 | Well/lease activity events — permits & completions (~23k) with status, operator, field and well attributes. |
| [`Activity_Production`](#prodmvestportal-activity-production) | 2,098,077 | 25 | Monthly production tied to activities/leases (~2.1M): oil/gas/BOE by cycle year-month. |
| [`Adjacent_Lease_Activity`](#prodmvestportal-adjacent-lease-activity) | 227,891 | 9 | Adjacent leases within a radius of each main lease (with status numbers). |
| [`Blog`](#prodmvestportal-blog) | 180 | 19 | CMS blog posts (category, content, SEO meta, publish flags). |
| [`Content_Data`](#prodmvestportal-content-data) | 114 | 5 | Per-county well & mineral content blocks. |
| [`Download_SampleData`](#prodmvestportal-download-sampledata) | 7 | 68 | Sample data payloads shown as download previews. |
| [`Feedback`](#prodmvestportal-feedback) | 7 | 12 | User feedback submissions (title, description, category, screenshot, status). |
| [`Glossary`](#prodmvestportal-glossary) | 42 | 18 | CMS glossary terms (definition, content, related terms, SEO). |
| [`LeaseLookup`](#prodmvestportal-leaselookup) | 240,875 | 4 | Lease name/number/district lookup index (~240k). |
| [`MVestimateCalculations`](#prodmvestportal-mvestimatecalculations) | 377,933 | 28 | Mineral-value (MVest) cashflow estimates (~378k): mvestimates plus down/mean/high cashflow sums vs yesterday. |
| [`Masterwell_data`](#prodmvestportal-masterwell-data) | 1 | 36 | Master well-type/status configuration lookup. |
| [`MonthlyDispositionVolumes`](#prodmvestportal-monthlydispositionvolumes) | 326,908 | 60 | Monthly lease disposition volumes (~327k), by county/playtype. |
| [`MonthlyProductionVolumes`](#prodmvestportal-monthlyproductionvolumes) | 385,254 | 76 | Monthly lease production volumes (~385k): oil/gas/cond/casinghead totals, operator, reservoir, playtype. |
| [`Newsnew`](#prodmvestportal-newsnew) | 11,638 | 53 | News articles (CMS) with categories, tags, county/field/operator links, likes. |
| [`Page_content`](#prodmvestportal-page-content) | 59,278 | 6 | Per-field/county page content blocks. |
| [`Presentation_FAQs`](#prodmvestportal-presentation-faqs) | 6 | 5 | FAQ content for presentations. |
| [`RRC_News`](#prodmvestportal-rrc-news) | 8 | 52 | Railroad Commission (RRC) news articles. |
| [`SystemGeneratedEmails`](#prodmvestportal-systemgeneratedemails) | 2,506 | 10 | System-generated email bodies/entities. |
| [`TexasProduction`](#prodmvestportal-texasproduction) | 258 | 24 | Per-county Texas production & reserves rollups (oil/gas/BOE produced & reserve, active/inactive leases & operators, rank, playtype). |
| [`popupEmailSubscribeUserEmails`](#prodmvestportal-popupemailsubscribeuseremails) | 198 | 4 | Emails captured via popup subscription (to, subject, HTML). |

## Database: `EF_SpAnalysis`

*Eagle Ford **spatial analysis** — lease production allocation, reserves time-series, nearest-neighbour lease relationships and per-lease well/geometry stats.*

### Collections index — 8 collection(s)

| Collection | Est. docs | Fields | Summary |
|---|---|---|---|
| [`Allocation`](#ef-spanalysis-allocation) | 24,454 | 155 | Per-well production-allocation monthly time-series (TSyyyymm columns from 2010 onward), keyed by API14 and lease. |
| [`Allocation_Reserves`](#ef-spanalysis-allocation-reserves) | 108,031 | 275 | Per-lease reserves-allocation monthly time-series (yyyymm columns) with forecast reserves. |
| [`Component_Allocation`](#ef-spanalysis-component-allocation) | 108,029 | 25 | Per-lease component allocation: historical & future oil/gas production, curve case and cycle indices. |
| [`Donuts`](#ef-spanalysis-donuts) | 67,804 | 17 | Per-lease 'donut' spatial statistics: directional frequency, mean age, acres-per-well and BOE-per-acre benchmarked against play and county percentiles. |
| [`Lease_Management`](#ef-spanalysis-lease-management) | 13,692 | 42 | Lease-management/allocation workflow records: production volumes, allocation batch/status and error-management flags per lease. |
| [`Neighbours_X2`](#ef-spanalysis-neighbours-x2) | 2,192,561 | 8 | Nearest-neighbour lease pairs (distance, rank, angle, direction) — ~2.2M relationships. |
| [`Neighbours_X2_NAMES`](#ef-spanalysis-neighbours-x2-names) | 2,192,561 | 10 | Nearest-neighbour lease pairs enriched with in/near lease names — ~2.2M relationships. |
| [`UniqueWellsPerLease`](#ef-spanalysis-uniquewellsperlease) | 1,032,805 | 43 | Unique wells per lease with full well-header attributes (API, location, operator, field, permit, dates). |

## Database: `Decline_data_to_web`

***Decline-curve analysis** outputs served to the web — per-well production history, Arps decline fits, EUR/reserves estimates and acreage/spacing data.*

### Collections index — 6 collection(s)

| Collection | Est. docs | Fields | Summary |
|---|---|---|---|
| [`AcreData_to_web_final`](#decline-data-to-web-acredata-to-web-final) | 504,297 | 14 | Per-well acreage/spacing dataset: acre-per-well (value, category, percentile), nearby mean, drilling activity, playtype and lat/long. |
| [`Data_raw_final`](#decline-data-to-web-data-raw-final) | 378,313 | 94 | Raw per-well decline-curve dataset: oil/gas production series, Arps predictions (upper/lower), GOR, change points, new-well probability, county/lease. |
| [`Data_raw_final_backup_2026`](#decline-data-to-web-data-raw-final-backup-2026) | 378,317 | 70 | 2026 backup copy of the raw per-well decline-curve dataset. |
| [`Data_to_web`](#decline-data-to-web-data-to-web) | 378,212 | 179 | Web-facing per-well summary (179 fields): EUR / produced / reserves (oil, gas, BOE), drilling activity, well density, new-well probability and connected-well information. |
| [`___Data_to_web_old`](#decline-data-to-web-data-to-web-old) | 17,475 | 113 | Older/superseded version of the web-facing per-well decline summary. |

## Database: `Operator_Directory`

*Operator directory datasets (operator profiles and related lookups).*

### Collections index — 3 collection(s)

| Collection | Est. docs | Fields | Summary |
|---|---|---|---|
| [`Operator_Detail_Section_Content`](#operator-directory-operator-detail-section-content) | 40 | 16 | CMS content sections for operator detail pages (intro, BOE, chart, heatmap, leases/wells sections). |

# MineralView MongoDB Database Architecture Documentation

## Overview

The MongoDB environment serves as the analytical and application-serving database for the MineralView platform. While PostgreSQL acts as the primary transactional and structured data repository, MongoDB is designed to store processed, analytical, and document-oriented datasets that power the MineralView website, map portal, production analytics, reserve estimation, community platform, operator directory, and AI-generated reporting systems.

Unlike PostgreSQL, which stores normalized relational data, MongoDB stores denormalized and precomputed documents optimized for high-performance read operations. This architecture minimizes backend processing time by allowing APIs to retrieve complete analytical datasets through a single database query.

The majority of the collections inside MongoDB contain information that has already been validated, cleaned, transformed, and analyzed through multiple ETL pipelines. These pipelines receive data from PostgreSQL, analytical models, and custom Python processing scripts before publishing the final documents to MongoDB.

The backend application primarily interacts with MongoDB to serve website requests, generate dashboards, display map analytics, perform production forecasting, retrieve reserve estimations, and provide users with highly optimized analytical insights.

The MongoDB server is divided into several specialized databases, each responsible for a specific business function within the MineralView ecosystem.

The primary MongoDB databases include:

1. Data Allocation
2. Decline Data To Web
3. EFSP Analysis
4. GeoMap Portal
5. Linkage Data
6. MineralView Community
7. Mineral Owner Info DB
8. Monthly Report DB
9. Operator Directory
10. ProdMvest Portal

Each database has been designed with a clear separation of responsibility, allowing different development teams—including Data Engineering, Backend Development, Analytics, and GIS—to manage their own datasets independently while maintaining overall system consistency.

---

## MongoDB Architecture Philosophy

### Why MongoDB?

The MineralView platform processes millions of production records, lease records, well records, ownership records, reserve calculations, spatial datasets, and analytical models. Many of these datasets require complex calculations before they can be presented to users.

Executing these calculations every time a user loads a webpage would significantly impact application performance.

To solve this problem, the platform adopts MongoDB as an analytical serving layer.

Instead of storing only raw information, MongoDB stores fully processed documents containing:

* Historical production
* Future production forecasts
* Reserve estimates
* BOE calculations
* Well performance scores
* Lease summaries
* Operator summaries
* Neighbor analysis
* Radius calculations
* AI-generated reports
* Community data
* Map visualization datasets

As a result, the backend simply retrieves a single document and sends it directly to the frontend without requiring expensive runtime joins or analytical computations.

---

## Overall MongoDB Workflow

The MongoDB environment operates as the final analytical layer within the MineralView data ecosystem.

The overall workflow consists of the following stages:

### Stage 1 – Data Collection

External information is collected from multiple sources, including:

* Texas Railroad Commission (RRC)
* County Appraisal Districts (CAD)
* Public GIS services
* Operator reports
* Production databases
* Internal analytical calculations

### Stage 2 – PostgreSQL Storage

The raw scraped datasets are initially stored inside PostgreSQL.

These datasets include:

* W1 permits
* W2 completions
* Mineral ownership
* Production history
* Market information
* GIS information
* Linkage datasets

### Stage 3 – Data Processing

Several ETL pipelines developed by the data engineering team process the PostgreSQL data.

These pipelines perform:

* Data validation
* Data cleaning
* Duplicate removal
* Production allocation
* Reserve calculations
* Decline curve analysis
* BOE calculations
* Forecast generation
* Well scoring
* Spatial analysis
* Neighbor calculations
* Operator aggregation

### Stage 4 – MongoDB Publication

Once the analytical processing has been completed, the final datasets are stored inside MongoDB.

MongoDB therefore contains only production-ready analytical documents.

### Stage 5 – Backend Consumption

The backend APIs query MongoDB directly to retrieve optimized analytical documents for:

* Website dashboards
* Well detail pages
* Lease detail pages
* Operator pages
* Community portal
* Monthly reports
* Interactive GIS maps
* Production analytics
* Reserve analysis

---

# 1. Data Allocation Database

## Purpose

The Data Allocation database stores well-level production allocation datasets generated through proprietary allocation models.

Unlike PostgreSQL, which stores production records in their original relational format, this database stores processed production allocations that have already undergone analytical calculations.

These datasets form the foundation for multiple downstream analytical systems throughout the MineralView platform.

## Business Objective

The primary objective of the Data Allocation database is to provide a centralized repository for well production allocations that can later be consumed by reserve calculations, production forecasting models, cash flow estimation systems, and production visualization dashboards.

The allocation process combines production history, lease information, well identifiers, BOE conversions, and allocation logic to determine production contributions at the well level.

## Collection Structure

The database primarily contains allocation documents representing:

* Historical oil production
* Historical gas production
* Historical BOE
* Future production forecasts
* Allocation percentages
* Well production summaries
* Time-series production values

The current active collection is a dated monthly snapshot (for example, the May 2026 allocation), which allows the platform to retain a versioned view of allocations as the underlying production data evolves month over month. Each document is keyed by the well API-14 identifier and carries the Arps decline parameters and the multi-step (W1–W4) forecast components used by the downstream models.

## Business Usage

The processed allocation data is subsequently used by:

* Reserve estimation models
* Decline curve analysis
* Production forecasting
* Well performance scoring
* Economic analysis
* Cash-flow calculations
* Backend reporting services

---

# 2. Decline Data To Web Database

## Purpose

The Decline Data To Web database stores the finished output of the platform's decline-curve analysis engine. It is the analytical layer that converts raw monthly production history into smoothed decline curves, Arps model fits, and the estimated ultimate recovery (EUR) numbers that users see on well and lease pages.

Where the Data Allocation database determines *how much* production belongs to each well, this database determines *how that production will behave over time* — both looking backward (history) and forward (forecast).

## Business Objective

The objective of this database is to precompute every value required to render a decline chart and its associated economics in the browser, so that no curve-fitting or forecasting has to happen at request time. A single document contains the entire story of a well: its production history, its fitted decline curve with upper and lower confidence bands, its remaining reserves, and metadata such as drilling activity and new-well probability.

## Collection Structure

The database contains a small number of large, wide collections that represent successive stages and versions of the decline pipeline:

* A **raw decline dataset** holding per-well production series (oil, gas, BOE), Arps predictions with upper/lower bounds, gas-oil ratio (GOR) lines, detected change points, and new-well probability.
* A **backup copy** of the raw dataset, retained for recovery and month-over-month comparison.
* A **web-facing summary dataset** — the widest collection — that carries the fully finished per-well figures presented on the site: EUR (oil / gas / BOE), produced and reserve volumes, drilling activity, mean age, well density, and connected-well information.
* An **older/superseded version** of the web-facing summary, retained for reference.
* An **acreage / spacing dataset** describing acre-per-well values, percentiles, nearby averages, playtype, and coordinates.
* An **error-report collection** capturing decline-processing diagnostics such as change points and minimum-point thresholds.

## Business Usage

This database directly powers:

* Well and lease decline-curve charts
* EUR and remaining-reserve displays
* Production history visualizations
* Acreage and spacing analytics
* Drilling-activity indicators
* Data-quality monitoring for the decline engine

---

# 3. EFSP Analysis Database

## Purpose

The EFSP Analysis (Eagle Ford Spatio-Temporal Analysis) database stores the spatial and time-series analytical datasets that describe how leases and wells relate to one another across geography and time. It is the engine behind neighbor analysis, lease allocation over time, and reserve time-series.

This database blends *temporal* analysis (production and reserves month by month) with *spatial* analysis (which leases are adjacent, in which direction, and at what distance).

## Business Objective

The goal of the EFSP Analysis database is to precompute expensive spatial and time-series relationships once, so the platform can instantly answer questions such as: "What are the nearest neighboring leases to this one?", "How have this lease's reserves changed over the last several years?", and "How does this lease compare to its play and county on acreage and BOE-per-acre?"

## Collection Structure

The database contains a focused set of analytical collections:

* **Allocation** — per-well production allocation as a monthly time-series (columns per year-month from 2010 onward), keyed by API-14 and lease.
* **Allocation Reserves** — per-lease reserves allocation as a monthly time-series with forecast reserves.
* **Component Allocation** — per-lease historical and future oil/gas production broken into components, with curve case and cycle indices.
* **Donuts** — per-lease "donut" spatial statistics: directional frequency, mean age, acres-per-well, and BOE-per-acre benchmarked against play and county percentiles.
* **Lease Management** — the allocation workflow layer: production volumes, allocation batch and status, and error-management flags per lease.
* **Neighbours X2** and **Neighbours X2 Names** — the nearest-neighbor lease relationships (distance, rank, angle, direction), one keyed purely by identifiers and one enriched with lease names. These are among the largest collections on the server (millions of relationship records each).
* **Unique Wells Per Lease** — the definitive list of unique wells belonging to each lease, with full well-header attributes.

## Business Usage

The EFSP Analysis datasets support:

* Neighbor and offset-well analysis
* Reserve time-series displays
* Lease-level spatial benchmarking
* Production allocation over time
* Analytical inputs for forecasting and valuation
* Map-based proximity features

---

# 4. GeoMap Portal Database

## Purpose

The GeoMap Portal database stores the geospatial datasets that render the interactive maps in the MineralView platform. It holds the well geometry used to draw wells and the lease-radius proximity data used for spatial search on the map.

## Business Objective

The objective of this database is to serve map-ready geometry and proximity information at very high speed, so the interactive map can plot wells, surface and bottom-hole locations, and nearby leases/permits without recomputing spatial relationships on each pan or zoom.

## Collection Structure

The database contains two primary collections:

* **Well Geo Data** — the well geometry/header master for the map: API identifiers, surface and bottom-hole coordinates, operator, lease, field, well status, and profile. This is a large collection covering well over a million wells.
* **Lease Radius Data** — per-lease radius proximity lists that precompute the nearby permits and leases within a given search radius around each main lease.

## Business Usage

This database powers:

* The interactive GIS map
* Well plotting and well-detail map popups
* Surface/bottom-hole location rendering
* Radius-based "what's near this lease" search
* Spatial context for lease and operator pages

---

# 5. Linkage Data Database

## Purpose

The Linkage Data database is the master cross-reference layer of the MongoDB environment. It ties together the many identities a single asset can have — well, lease, mineral owner, permit, and production record — into wide, self-contained documents. It is the MongoDB counterpart of the PostgreSQL `Linkage_data` schema.

## Business Objective

The objective of this database is to eliminate expensive multi-table joins at request time by pre-joining all of the linked information about a well/lease into a single document. When a user opens a well or lease, the backend can retrieve one linkage document that already contains the well identifiers, lease details, ownership, permit references, survey/location, and production linkage.

## Collection Structure

The database is organized **by county and year**. Each Texas county has its own dedicated collection for the current cycle (the 2026 series), and every document is very wide — roughly ninety or more fields — because it consolidates data that would otherwise live across many relational tables.

A typical county linkage document includes:

* Well identifiers (API-14, tracking numbers, status numbers)
* Lease information (number, name, district, county, acreage)
* Field and reservoir information
* Operator information
* Permit and filing information (type, purpose, dates)
* Survey and location coordinates (surface and bottom-hole)
* Directional survey references and paths
* Production and test linkage
* Play type and status
* Timestamps for creation, update, and synchronization

Because there are 254 Texas counties (plus a handful of secondary/variant collections), this database contains the largest number of collections on the server — 259 in total — even though each one shares the same document shape.

## Business Usage

The linkage datasets support:

* Well detail pages
* Lease detail pages
* Ownership-to-well resolution
* Permit and completion cross-referencing
* County-level data delivery
* Data exports and downloads

---

# 6. MineralView Community Database

## Purpose

The MineralView Community database powers the platform's social and collaboration layer — the community portal where users read and post news, participate in public and private groups, create threads, and receive notifications. This is an application-serving database (document-oriented app data) rather than an analytical one.

## Business Objective

The objective of this database is to store all user-generated community content and its supporting structures — groups, threads, posts, bookmarks, notifications, and user profiles — in a document model that maps naturally to how the community frontend renders content.

## Collection Structure

The database contains collections grouped into several functional areas:

**Groups and threads**

* Public group listings and public group threads
* Private groups, private group members, and private group threads
* Thread details (posts, votes, links, SEO metadata, summaries)
* MVP group structures (invites, groups, threads) reserved for a group feature

**News and content**

* Texas news and archived Texas news
* County news and archived county news

**User engagement**

* User profiles (bio, job role, credentials, post/like/answer counts)
* Bookmarks and thread-specific bookmarks
* Notifications, notification settings, and notification users
* Recent-activity feeds (public and private)

**Search support**

* A trigram word index over questions that powers community search and suggestions

**Membership**

* Community members (a lightweight mirror of the platform's app members)

Some of these collections — particularly several private-group and MVP-group structures — are currently empty, reflecting features that are provisioned but not yet populated.

## Business Usage

This database supports:

* The community/forum experience
* Public and private group collaboration
* Threaded discussions and posts
* Community news feeds
* User profiles and reputation counters
* In-app notifications and subscriptions
* Community search

---

# 7. Mineral Owner Info DB

## Purpose

The Mineral Owner Info database stores the platform's mineral-ownership master data in a form optimized for fast lookup and search. It is one of the highest-volume databases on the server, holding millions of ownership records.

## Business Objective

The objective of this database is to make mineral-owner information instantly searchable and retrievable — both the detailed ownership records themselves and the search-optimized aggregates that power autocomplete and owner-lookup features.

## Collection Structure

The database contains two complementary collections:

* **Mineral Owners Data** — the master ownership records (several million documents). Each document describes an owner's interest: owner number, name, address, and city; county, lease number and name, and district code; interest type and value; appraised value; current operator; play type; and map-data flags. Both current and prior ("old") name/address variants are retained.
* **Mineral Owners Details** — an aggregated, search-oriented view (hundreds of thousands of documents). Each document rolls up an owner's counties, operators, and lease names, and stores prefix/token search fields plus exact/prefix/token search keys designed to drive fast autocomplete and typeahead search.

## Business Usage

This database powers:

* Mineral-owner search and autocomplete
* Owner detail pages
* Ownership-to-lease resolution
* Appraised-value and interest displays
* Owner-centric analytics and exports

---

# 8. Monthly Report DB

## Purpose

The Monthly Report database manages the generation and delivery of the platform's automated monthly reports to subscribed members.

## Business Objective

The objective of this database is to track which members receive monthly reports and to store the metadata for each generated report — where it lives, which month it covers, how many leases it summarizes, and whether it has been delivered.

## Collection Structure

The database contains two collections:

* **Members** — the members enrolled to receive monthly reports (member id, type, name, email, registration date).
* **Monthly Reports** — the generated report records: report URL, report month, lease count, creation and sent dates, report status, download flags, and whether the notification email was sent.

## Business Usage

This database supports:

* Automated monthly report generation
* Report delivery tracking
* Member report subscriptions
* Email/notification auditing for reports

---

# 9. Operator Directory

## Purpose

The Operator Directory database stores the datasets that power the platform's operator pages — the profiles, production summaries, and filterable datasets that let users explore oil and gas operators.

## Business Objective

The objective of this database is to precompute operator-level rollups and page content so that operator pages load complete summaries in a single query, and so operators can be filtered and searched across their leases and production.

## Collection Structure

The database contains three collections:

* **Operator Production Summary Yearly** — yearly production summaries per operator: lease counts, total oil/gas/BOE, producing counties, production date ranges, and quarter-over-quarter comparisons.
* **Operators Filter Data Final** — a large per-lease operator dataset (over a million documents) used for operator filtering and search, including county, lease, production totals, date ranges, and SEO-friendly operator names/URLs.
* **Operator Detail Section Content** — the CMS content sections that make up an operator detail page (intro, BOE, chart, heatmap, operated-leases, and operated-wells sections).

## Business Usage

This database powers:

* Operator directory and operator detail pages
* Operator search and filtering
* Operator production summaries and rankings
* Operator-level SEO landing pages

---

# 10. ProdMvest Portal Database

## Purpose

The ProdMvest Portal database is the broad application-serving database behind the production and mineral-value-estimate (MVest) portal. It combines analytical production datasets, valuation estimates, activity feeds, and a large amount of website content and CMS material.

## Business Objective

The objective of this database is to serve, from a single place, everything the production/valuation portal needs — precomputed monthly production and disposition volumes, mineral-value cashflow estimates, lease and activity lookups, and all of the editorial and support content that surrounds them.

## Collection Structure

The database contains collections spanning several functional areas:

**Production and valuation analytics**

* Monthly Production Volumes and Monthly Disposition Volumes (per-lease, hundreds of thousands of documents)
* Activity Production — monthly production tied to activity/lease records (millions of documents)
* MVestimate Calculations — mineral-value cashflow estimates with down/mean/high scenarios versus prior day
* Texas Production — per-county Texas production and reserve rollups with rankings

**Activity and lookups**

* Activity — well/lease activity events (permits and completions) with status and attributes
* Adjacent Lease Activity — adjacent leases within a radius of each main lease
* Lease Lookup — lease name/number/district lookup index
* Masterwell Data — master well-type/status configuration

**Content and CMS**

* Blog, Glossary, Newsnew, and RRC News — editorial and news content
* Content Data and Page Content — per-county and per-field page content blocks
* Presentation FAQs and Download Sample Data — supporting content and sample previews

**User interaction and system**

* Feedback — user feedback submissions
* System Generated Emails — system email bodies/templates
* Popup Email Subscribe User Emails — emails captured through subscription popups

## Business Usage

This database powers:

* Production and disposition dashboards
* Mineral-value (MVest) estimates and cashflow displays
* Activity feeds and lease/well detail context
* Operator, county, and Texas-wide production summaries
* Blog, glossary, news, and other website content
* User feedback and system email delivery

---

# Overall MongoDB Data Flow

The MongoDB environment sits at the end of the MineralView data pipeline and can be understood as a set of cooperating layers:

**Ingestion and staging (PostgreSQL)**

Raw scraped and collected data — permits, completions, ownership, production, GIS, and market information — lands first in PostgreSQL, which acts as the system of record.

**Processing (ETL and analytical models)**

Python-based ETL pipelines and analytical models read from PostgreSQL, then validate, clean, deduplicate, allocate production, fit decline curves, compute reserves and BOE, run spatial and neighbor analysis, and aggregate operators.

**Analytical serving (MongoDB)**

The finished, denormalized documents are published to the specialized MongoDB databases described above. Each database owns a distinct slice of the product:

* **Data Allocation** and **Decline Data To Web** own production allocation and decline/forecast analytics.
* **EFSP Analysis** and **GeoMap Portal** own spatial/temporal relationships and map geometry.
* **Linkage Data** owns the wide, county-partitioned cross-reference documents.
* **Mineral Owner Info DB** owns ownership master and search data.
* **Operator Directory** and **ProdMvest Portal** own operator, production, valuation, and website/CMS content.
* **MineralView Community** owns the social/collaboration layer.
* **Monthly Report DB** owns automated reporting.

**Consumption (backend APIs and frontend)**

Backend APIs read directly from MongoDB, retrieving complete documents that are sent to the frontend with little or no additional computation. This is what allows well pages, lease pages, operator pages, maps, dashboards, and the community portal to load quickly despite the scale of the underlying data.

---

# MongoDB Best Practices

The MineralView MongoDB environment follows several practices that keep this analytical serving layer fast, reliable, and maintainable:

* **Separation of responsibility.** Each database owns a single business domain, so teams can evolve their datasets independently without affecting unrelated features.
* **Denormalized, read-optimized documents.** Data is pre-joined and precomputed so that the common case — reading one document to render a page — is as cheap as possible.
* **Versioned and dated snapshots.** Dated collections (such as monthly allocation snapshots and dated backups) preserve point-in-time views and support month-over-month comparison and recovery.
* **County/time partitioning for very wide data.** The linkage layer is partitioned by county and cycle year, keeping individual collections focused and predictable in shape.
* **Search-optimized companion collections.** High-volume lookup data (such as mineral ownership) is paired with aggregated, tokenized collections that back fast autocomplete and search.
* **Indexing on primary access paths.** Collections are indexed on the identifiers used to fetch them (for example, owner name, lease, and API-based keys) so that reads stay fast at scale.
* **Clear provenance from PostgreSQL.** Because MongoDB holds processed output, the raw source of every dataset remains traceable back through the ETL pipelines to PostgreSQL and the original external sources.

---

# MongoDB Architecture Summary

The MongoDB ecosystem is the analytical and application-serving backbone of the MineralView platform. Where PostgreSQL captures and stores the raw, structured record of the business, MongoDB stores the *finished product*: denormalized, precomputed documents that the website and APIs can serve directly.

By dividing responsibility across specialized databases — production allocation, decline analytics, spatio-temporal analysis, map geometry, county linkage, mineral-owner search, operator directory, production/valuation portal, community, and reporting — the platform achieves a clean separation between raw data and analytical output, and between analytical output and the different product surfaces that consume it.

The result is an architecture in which heavy computation happens once, in controlled ETL and analytical pipelines, and is then delivered to users as fast, single-query reads. This design lets MineralView present millions of production, lease, well, and ownership records — along with reserve estimates, forecasts, spatial analysis, and community content — with the responsiveness of a modern web application.
